system_prompt:
You are extracting the core signal from a GitHub issue or pull request for duplicate detection. The output will be embedded and clustered with similar issues.

Your job is to answer: "What is this issue/PR fundamentally about?" Ignore everything else.

Strip away ALL of the following — they add noise to embeddings:
- Template boilerplate (headings like "### Steps to reproduce", "### Expected behavior")
- Testing notes, QA instructions, review checklists
- Environment details (OS, browser, versions) unless they ARE the core issue
- Deployment procedures, rollback plans
- Links, references to other issues
- Formatting artifacts (checkboxes, horizontal rules, badges)

What to capture:
- The specific bug, feature, or change in concrete terms
- Which component/module/API/subsystem is affected
- Why it matters (the user impact, not the testing impact)

Return JSON with keys problem_summary, solution_summary, maintainer_signal_summary, dedupe_summary.
Rules: plain text only, no markdown, 1-3 sentences each.
dedupe_summary is the most important field — it must be a clean, specific description that would match other reports about the same underlying issue.

results.title: Title channels.discord.proxy does not proxy REST API requests — guild resolve fails, guild channels broken behind HTTP proxy

summary.problem_summary: channels.discord.proxy only applies to the Discord WebSocket Gateway; REST API calls (command deploy, fetch bot identity) and guild resolution do not use the configured HTTP proxy, causing startup REST timeouts and unresolved guild channels so the bot is unresponsive in guilds while DMs work.
summary.solution_summary: Apply the configured proxy to all HTTP/REST requests (set undici's ProxyAgent/global dispatcher for REST and guild-resolve code paths) or add a global proxy configuration that installs a ProxyAgent for all network calls; ensure guild resolve uses the proxied path and add retry logic.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression: worked before but now Node.js built-in fetch/undici doesn't respect HTTP_PROXY, and current code only proxies the Gateway WS. A workaround (setGlobalDispatcher) fixes REST but not guild resolve, indicating a separate code path; tests and fixes should target REST/guild-resolve startup paths and consider a global proxy option.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord channel proxy config only proxies the WebSocket gateway, not REST API calls or guild resolution, causing REST timeouts and unresolved guild channels behind an HTTP proxy.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core bug (proxy applies to WebSocket but not REST/guild resolution) without boilerplate or irrelevant details, so it will cluster with similar reports.

results.title: [Bug]:channels.discord.proxy doesn’t apply to outbound REST sends / Carbon RequestClient fetch has no proxy support

summary.problem_summary: Outbound Discord REST requests (message sends/attachments) ignore the configured channels.discord.proxy and fail with TypeError: fetch failed, while the WebSocket gateway (receiving) works. The Carbon RequestClient/fetch implementation currently has no proxy support, so bots behind network proxies (e.g., China) cannot send messages or upload/download media.
summary.solution_summary: Add proxy support to the Discord REST client by making RequestClient/RequestHandler pass a proxy agent (HTTP/SOCKS/TUN) or honor channels.discord.proxy for all outgoing HTTP requests, including message sends and attachment uploads/downloads. Ensure REST fetches use the same proxy behavior as the gateway and provide config-driven proxy handling.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: User reports logs indicate "rest proxy enabled" but REST requests still bypass the proxy; WebSocket receives function normally. Affected OpenClaw version 2026.2.26; change required in Carbon's RequestClient/fetch integration to attach a proxy agent or use a proxy-aware fetch implementation so REST traffic routes through the configured proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST requests bypass the configured channels.discord.proxy because the Carbon RequestClient/fetch has no proxy support, causing outgoing message sends and attachment transfers to fail behind proxies.

judge.reasoning: The summary succinctly states the core bug and root cause with precise technical terms (channels.discord.proxy, Carbon RequestClient/fetch, REST requests) and no extraneous template or testing details, so it will cluster with similar proxy-related REST failures.

results.title: Discord can log in behind proxy, but native command deploy / bot identity fetch fail with 'fetch failed'

summary.problem_summary: When OpenClaw's Discord provider is configured to use an HTTP proxy it can successfully connect the gateway/WebSocket and fetch the application ID, but startup REST calls (native command deployment and bot identity fetch) consistently fail with 'fetch failed', leaving the provider half-working.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure all Discord REST requests use the same proxy-aware HTTP fetch/dispatcher as the gateway (unify transport/proxy handling), so startup REST calls are routed through the configured proxy and include consistent retry/error handling.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show 'rest proxy enabled' and 'gateway proxy enabled', application ID fetch succeeds, but deploy-rest:put and fetch-bot-identity return 'fetch failed' despite 'logged in to discord'. Issue reproducible with channels.discord.proxy configured; OpenClaw versions 2026.3.12/2026.3.13 reported. Likely different code paths for gateway vs REST requests.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord provider connects via proxy to the gateway but some startup REST calls (PUT /applications/.../commands and bot identity fetch) fail with 'fetch failed' — likely due to inconsistent or missing proxy handling for certain REST request paths.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that identifies the core failure (startup REST calls for command deployment and bot identity fetch failing with 'fetch failed' despite gateway login) and points to a likely cause (inconsistent/missing proxy handling) which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord proxy config recognized but REST API fails with fetch failed

summary.problem_summary: Discord channel proxy is logged as enabled but Discord REST API calls fail with "fetch failed" and the gateway WebSocket never connects when running OpenClaw under WSL2 with an HTTP proxy configured (channels.discord.proxy). This renders the Discord channel non-functional despite the proxy being reachable via curl.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the configured HTTP proxy is actually applied to the Discord.js REST/fetch client and WebSocket gateway (use a proxy agent or set fetch/HTTP client to honor channels.discord.proxy), so REST requests and gateway connections go through the proxy in WSL2 and similar environments.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show "rest proxy enabled" and "gateway proxy enabled" but subsequent REST operations error with "fetch failed" and gatewayConnected=false; curl via the proxy succeeds and Feishu works, so the proxy itself is fine. Affects users requiring HTTP proxy (WSL2); reproducible and high severity; config stored at ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json → channels.discord.proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord proxy config is recognized but not actually applied to the Discord.js REST/fetch (and gateway) calls, causing "fetch failed" and gateway connection failures when an HTTP proxy is required (e.g., WSL2).

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core problem — proxy config is detected but not applied to Discord.js REST/gateway calls causing 'fetch failed' — without extraneous template or repro details.

results.title: Discord REST API ignores proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls from the OpenClaw gateway ignore channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY environment variables and fail with 'TypeError: fetch failed', while the Discord WebSocket and other channels (e.g., Telegram) work correctly.
summary.solution_summary: Route Discord REST API requests through the same proxy-aware fetch/SSRF-guard path used for web_search/web_fetch so channels.discord.proxy and HTTP(S)_PROXY are honored; update the Discord channel code to use the shared proxy-aware fetch implementation.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show 'discord final reply failed: TypeError: fetch failed'. User provided config, env vars, and reproduction steps; curl through the proxy to Discord API succeeds, and changelog notes web tools were fixed but Discord REST was excluded. User attempted both channel config and env vars and reports a webhook workaround only for push, not replies.
summary.dedupe_summary: OpenClaw's Discord REST API requests are not routed through the configured proxy/HTTP(S)_PROXY and fail with 'fetch failed'.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary stating that Discord REST requests aren't using the configured proxy (HTTP(S)_PROXY) and fail with 'fetch failed', which captures the core bug for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI 'openclaw agent --channel discord --to' routes to agent:*:main instead of live channel session

summary.problem_summary: The CLI command openclaw agent --channel discord --to resolves and sends messages to the generic agent:*:main session instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the session resolver does not look up sessions by delivery context (channel + to + accountId) and Discord targets are not normalized.
summary.solution_summary: Update the agent CLI session resolution to search by real delivery context (channel + to + accountId), normalize Discord target identifiers (e.g., prepend channel:), ensure the command path passes channel and replyAccount/accountId to the resolver, and fix compact resolver lookup logic to use the correct main-session builder.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Manual hotfixes were applied to compiled dist files (dist/gateway-cli-*.js, dist/compact-*.js, dist/register.agent-*.js) to add delivery-context lookup and normalization; changes are high-risk and will be lost on reinstall. Commit an equivalent fix in source, run post-update revalidation, and review the compact resolver and command-path propagation to avoid regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: openclaw CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread because the session resolver omits lookup by delivery context (channel+to+accountId) and Discord target normalization is missing.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that states the CLI command, observed misrouting to agent:*:main, and the precise root causes (missing delivery-context lookup and Discord target normalization), which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads - volatile metadata re-injection + CLI routing to wrong session

summary.problem_summary: Two regressions in OpenClaw's Discord integration: 1) volatile per-message metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) was being re-injected into thread sessions every turn, causing progressive context bloat and degraded behavior; 2) CLI agent commands targeted to a Discord thread routed to the default main session (agent:*:main) because the session resolver did not lookup by actual delivery context (channel+to+accountId).
summary.solution_summary: Prevent re-injection of specified volatile metadata for Discord threads; update CLI/gateway session resolution to normalize Discord targets and resolve sessions by delivery context (channel + to + accountId); pass channel and replyAccount/accountId into the resolver and replace incorrect resolver usage with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fixes were applied directly to compiled runtime bundles in openclaw/dist/ (pi-embedded, gateway-cli, compact, register.agent) and verified by restarting the gateway service and observing corrected writer/reasoning sessions and no repeated thread context; manual patching of dist/ files is required and will be lost on reinstall, so re-application script/update is needed.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord thread sessions repeatedly receive volatile metadata each turn (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, chat-history) causing context bloat, and CLI agent commands route to the wrong session because the resolver doesn't resolve by delivery context (channel+to+accountId).

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: captures the two core regressions (repeated injection of volatile Discord thread metadata causing context bloat, and CLI routing to wrong session due to resolver not using delivery context), with no boilerplate or irrelevant detail.

results.title: [Bug]: tools/sandbox: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES (5 MB) is hardcoded and not user-configurable

summary.problem_summary: The sandbox media staging limit is hardcoded as MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5 * 1024 * 1024 inside dist/store-*.js, causing any files over 5MB to be rejected or silently fail when copying into sandboxed agents (sandbox.mode: "non-main").
summary.solution_summary: Make the MEDIA_MAX_BYTES value configurable via openclaw.json (e.g., tools.media.maxBytes) or honor existing per-type media limits before staging; remove the hardcoded constant in the distributed build and read the limit from runtime config with a sensible default.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression present since at least v2026.3.7 and still in v2026.3.8; users currently must patch dist/store-*.js after npm install to raise the cap (sed workaround). Affects npm-installed distribution and causes silent failures for >5MB files in sandboxed agents.
summary.dedupe_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES hardcoded to 5MB in dist/store-*.js prevents staging files >5MB into sandboxed agents and there is no openclaw.json config to override it.

judge.reasoning: The summary concisely and specifically states the core bug (hardcoded MEDIA_MAX_BYTES = 5MB in dist/store-*.js blocking sandbox file staging) and that there's no openclaw.json override, which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord REST API requests fail with "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration being enabled

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw's Discord provider fails all REST API requests with "fetch failed" even though the REST proxy is configured and logged as enabled; the Gateway WebSocket works and the bot can receive messages but cannot send replies or deploy commands.
summary.solution_summary: Update the Discord REST client to actually use the configured proxy for fetch calls (e.g., attach an HTTP(S) proxy agent or honor proxy env vars) so REST requests are routed through the proxy like curl and the Gateway are.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Logs show "rest proxy enabled" and gateway proxy enabled, WebSocket connection and inbound messages work, and direct curl with the same proxy succeeds, but native command deployment and other REST calls fail with "fetch failed"; indicates the REST proxy implementation or fetch usage isn't honoring the proxy configuration.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests error with "fetch failed" despite rest proxy being enabled in OpenClaw; WebSocket works and curl with the proxy succeeds, implying the Discord provider's REST client isn't using the configured proxy and prevents sending messages.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that identifies REST API calls failing with 'fetch failed' despite proxy config, notes WebSocket/curl workarounds, and pinpoints the likely cause (Discord REST client not using the proxy), which is ideal for deduplication.

results.title: Discord REST API (@buape/carbon) ignores channels.discord.proxy config

summary.problem_summary: Discord REST API calls made via @buape/carbon's RequestClient ignore the channels.discord.proxy setting in openclaw.json and call globalThis.fetch without a proxy-aware dispatcher; as a result REST requests (and other fetch paths like memorySearch and attachment downloads) bypass the configured HTTP proxy while the WebSocket gateway uses it.
summary.solution_summary: Change @buape/carbon's RequestClient (and other fetch code paths) to accept and use an explicit dispatcher/proxy option and have OpenClaw pass channels.discord.proxy into those REST/fetch calls, or set a proxy-aware undici dispatcher for native fetch so Node's fetch routes through the configured proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Update RequestClient API to accept a dispatcher/proxy and wire OpenClaw channels.discord.proxy into REST calls; audit memorySearch and fetchRemoteMedia to use the same proxy handling; avoid using NODE_OPTIONS=--use-env-proxy as it breaks WebSocket connections.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord REST API requests via @buape/carbon RequestClient bypass the configured channels.discord.proxy (globalThis.fetch used without proxy dispatcher), so REST/fetch traffic is not routed through the HTTP proxy while the WebSocket gateway is.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies @buape/carbon RequestClient using globalThis.fetch and bypassing channels.discord.proxy so REST calls aren't routed through the HTTP proxy, matching similar reports accurately.

results.title: [Bug]: Performance degradation - context growth + lane blocking under load

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw degrades under sustained load with long-running conversations: context grows without bounds (writer ~42k tokens, reasoning ~90k) causing rising per-turn latency; long turns block Discord lanes and cause serialization under load; typing indicators can remain stuck until TTL in some code paths.
summary.solution_summary: Applied configuration limits and timeouts: reduced concurrency (acp.maxConcurrentSessions 12→6, agents.defaults.maxConcurrent 3→2, subagents maxConcurrent 8→4, maxChildrenPerAgent 8→4), set agents.defaults.contextTokens=80000, increased channels.discord.eventQueue.listenerTimeout 120000→300000, improved patching script to handle hash variants, and restarted the gateway.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Root causes are missing effective context token limits, overly high concurrency saturating lanes, and insufficient event queue timeouts; restart mitigates but does not eliminate the problem. Recommended long-term fixes: enforce/adapt context token caps, tighten concurrency controls, increase event-queue timeouts, and expose these as tunable config options.
summary.dedupe_summary: Performance degradation and Discord lane blocking during sustained long conversations caused by unbounded context token growth and excessive concurrency, leading to serialization and stuck typing indicators; remedy via context token limits, lower concurrency, and longer event-queue listener timeouts.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that names the root technical causes (unbounded context token growth, excessive concurrency) and observable impacts (Discord lane blocking, serialization, stuck typing), with suggested mitigations — no boilerplate and good for clustering similar issues.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread context re-injected every turn (context pollution + cache bust)

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11, Discord thread handling repeatedly injects per-message volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, "Chat history since last reply") on every conversation turn, causing context contamination, token bloat, performance degradation, and prompt cache invalidation.
summary.solution_summary: Add an isNewSession guard so thread-starter/history and volatile IDs/timestamps are injected only on the first turn, correct session key generation to use buildAgentMainSessionKey(...), and implement the fix in source (not just compiled dist) with post-update validation.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: A manual hotpatch was applied to compiled files (dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js and dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js) adding an isNewSession guard and replacing resolveAgentMainSessionKey with buildAgentMainSessionKey; this is high-risk because the changes live only in dist and will be lost on updates, so a source-level fix and automated revalidation are required.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads re-inject per-message volatile metadata every turn (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, chat history) due to a missing isNewSession guard, causing context pollution and prompt cache busting.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that identifies the exact symptom (per-turn injection of volatile Discord thread metadata), root cause (missing isNewSession guard), and impact (context pollution and cache busting), making it ideal for deduplication.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Discord thread metadata re-injected every turn (context pollution)

summary.problem_summary: Discord thread messages repeatedly have volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history since last reply) re-injected on every turn, causing context duplication, degraded performance in long threads, increased token costs, and prompt-cache busting. The issue originates from the compiled runtime handling of Discord threads in dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js which lacks a guard to only inject that metadata on the first turn.
summary.solution_summary: A manual patch to dist/pi-embedded-D6PpOsxP.js was applied to stop injecting volatile Discord thread metadata after the first turn, restoring prompt caching and reducing token usage; recommended permanent fix is to implement the guard in source code and add revalidation post-update.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: High-risk regression: current fix is a manual edit to compiled dist files and will be lost on updates; maintainers should apply the fix in source, add a test/guard to ensure metadata injection happens only on the first thread turn, and include this in CI to prevent future regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord threads repeatedly re-inject per-message volatile metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, Chat history) on every turn, duplicating context and busting prompt cache due to missing first-turn injection guard in the compiled runtime (dist/pi-embedded-*.js).

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that names the exact metadata re-injected, the root cause (missing first-turn guard), and the compiled runtime file pattern, so it will accurately cluster similar reports.

results.title: [Bug]: Discord threads root fix - runtime patches for context growth + session routing

summary.problem_summary: Discord threads in the OpenClaw runtime exhibit two regressions: per-turn re-injection of message metadata (message_id, reply_to_id, timestamp, Thread starter, InboundHistory) causing progressive context growth and latency, and the session resolver falling back to agent:*:main instead of resolving an existing live thread-bound session, causing CLI commands to route to the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: Applied runtime patches to compiled dist JS bundles to stop repeated metadata reinjection and to make session resolution use delivery context (channel + to + accountId); added idempotent re-apply script and backups, restarted and validated the gateway.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Fix was applied manually to compiled bundles (dist/*.js) and is non-persistent across installs—backups (*.bak-*) and an idempotent reapplication script exist; gateway restarted and logs verified; upstream permanent fix and regression tests should be implemented to avoid repeated manual patches.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord-thread regression: per-turn repeated metadata injection causing unbounded context growth and latency, plus session resolver falling back to agent:*:main instead of resolving thread-bound sessions, resulting in wrong CLI/session routing.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it names the two technical regressions (per-turn metadata causing context growth/latency and session resolver falling back to agent:*:main instead of thread-bound sessions) with no boilerplate, so it will cluster correctly.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord messages not being sent - "fetch failed" despite proxy configuration

summary.problem_summary: OpenClaw cannot send reply messages to Discord: sending fails with "TypeError: fetch failed" even though Discord proxy settings are enabled and the bot is logged in; receiving messages and model responses work fine.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the Discord REST send-message path respects the configured proxy (use a proxy agent or honor HTTP_PROXY/HTTPS_PROXY for Node fetch) and surface the underlying fetch error; update fetch usage to attach the proxy agent or global proxy settings so replies route through the proxy.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression observed in 2026.3.2: logs show "discord: rest proxy enabled" and "discord: gateway proxy enabled" and successful login, but final reply fails with "TypeError: fetch failed"; curl through the same proxy succeeds, and the issue affects both DMs and group chats, indicating the REST send code likely isn't applying the proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: OpenClaw cannot send Discord reply messages because the REST send path fails with "fetch failed" despite proxy being enabled and working elsewhere, indicating the message-send fetch call is not using the configured proxy.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that identifies the core failure (Discord REST send path failing with 'fetch failed' despite proxy config) and the likely cause (send call not using the proxy), with no template noise.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: CLI agent --channel discord --to routes to agent:*:main instead of live session

summary.problem_summary: openclaw agent --channel discord --to ... routes messages to agent:*:main instead of the live Discord channel/thread session because the CLI session resolver does not lookup sessions by delivery context and does not normalize Discord target IDs.
summary.solution_summary: Modify the session resolver to normalize Discord targets (e.g., prepend channel:) and perform a session lookup by delivery context (channel + to + accountId) before falling back to agent:*:main; the reporter patched the compiled dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js manually with this fix.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: The bug is in the compiled artifact dist/gateway-cli-BjsM6fWb.js; a manual edit was made to dist to add normalization and delivery-context lookup, but this is high-regression risk and will be lost on reinstall—fix must be applied in source and released properly.
summary.dedupe_summary: CLI agent routing to Discord channels falls back to agent:*:main because the session resolver fails to normalize Discord target IDs and doesn't lookup sessions by delivery context (channel+to+accountId).

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states the CLI routes to agent:*:main because the session resolver doesn't normalize Discord target IDs or lookup by delivery context (channel+to+accountId).

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Regression in compact - resolveAgentMainSessionKey ReferenceError

summary.problem_summary: A regression in OpenClaw v2026.3.11: the compiled file dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls a nonexistent function resolveAgentMainSessionKey, causing ReferenceError at runtime, breaking the agent command and potentially crashing the gateway when processing agent commands (session resolver patch introduced the wrong function).
summary.solution_summary: Manually patch dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js to replace resolveAgentMainSessionKey(...) with buildAgentMainSessionKey(...); this removes the ReferenceError and restores agent/session behavior. Note this is a high-risk fix because it modifies compiled files and will be lost on reinstall unless fixed in source.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a regression introduced by the earlier session resolver fix (#3); maintainers should update the source patch to use buildAgentMainSessionKey instead of resolveAgentMainSessionKey so compiled bundles do not contain the invalid call. The issue is urgent because the manual fix in dist/ will be overwritten by updates.
summary.dedupe_summary: Compiled bundle dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js calls undefined resolveAgentMainSessionKey instead of buildAgentMainSessionKey, causing ReferenceError and breaking agent commands in OpenClaw v2026.3.11; requires replacing the call in the compiled file or fixing the source patch.

judge.reasoning: Summary clearly states the concrete regression (wrong function call in compiled bundle causing ReferenceError and broken agent commands) but includes a version tag and a remediation note which are minor noise for deduping.

results.title: [Bug]:  Discord plugin fails with "fetch failed" when using proxy

summary.problem_summary: Discord plugin's REST API requests ignore the configured channels.discord.proxy (only WebSocket connections use it), causing health-checks to report 'fetch failed' and message sends to time out in environments that require an HTTP proxy.
summary.solution_summary: Ensure the Discord plugin's HTTP client uses channels.discord.proxy for all REST API requests and health checks so REST calls are routed through the configured proxy; add tests and update health-check logic to use the proxied client.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Regression observed in OpenClaw 2026.3.8 (commit 3caab92). Repro: set channels.discord.proxy, verify curl via proxy succeeds, then run openclaw message send or health check which fails with 'fetch failed' while WebSocket proxying works—indicating REST calls bypass the proxy.
summary.dedupe_summary: Discord channel REST API requests ignore channels.discord.proxy (only WebSocket uses the proxy), causing fetch failed/timeouts behind an HTTP proxy.

judge.reasoning: Concise and focused: it states the core bug (REST API ignores channels.discord.proxy while WebSocket uses it) with technical terms and impact (fetch failures/timeouts), no boilerplate or extraneous detail.

results.title: [Bug] v2026.3.11: Session resolver in compact lacks delivery-context lookup

summary.problem_summary: In OpenClaw v2026.3.11 the compiled session resolver for the agent command (dist/compact-1mmJ_KWL.js) does not perform a delivery-context lookup (channel + to + accountId), so sessions fall back to agent:*:main instead of resolving to the live session for the target channel/thread. This breaks routing and causes agent responses to be associated with the wrong session.
summary.solution_summary: Implement delivery-context lookup in the agent command's session resolver: search for an existing session by channel + to + accountId and only fall back to agent:*:main when no specific session is found; apply the change in source code and rebuild (not just edit compiled dist files), and add tests to cover delivery-context resolution.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a production-impact bug in compiled code; the correct fix must be applied in the source and included in the build pipeline because manual edits to dist/compact-*.js are high-risk and will be overwritten by upgrades. Prioritize a source-level fix, add tests, and release a rebuild to prevent regressions.
summary.dedupe_summary: Agent command session resolver (dist/compact-*.js) lacks delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId), causing sessions to default to agent:*:main instead of the target channel/thread session.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies the session resolver in dist/compact-*.js missing delivery-context lookup (channel+to+accountId) and the resulting incorrect fallback to agent:*:main, with no boilerplate or noise.

results.title: [Feature Request] Add i18n support for OpenClaw Control UI (Chinese localization)

summary.problem_summary: The OpenClaw Control UI has most interface text hardcoded in English (settings, buttons, agent/skill pages, channel configs, status messages, tooltips), creating usability barriers for non-English users—notably Chinese speakers; the distributed control-ui bundle contains these hardcoded strings.
summary.solution_summary: Extract all hardcoded UI strings into an i18n layer (e.g., JSON locale files), provide a zh-CN locale with translations, add a language selector or auto-detect system language, and build infrastructure to easily add more languages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This is a feature request (priority medium-high) noting docs already have zh-CN translations and the author asks whether this is on the roadmap and if contributors are welcome; implementation requires changing control-ui bundles to use locale files.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add i18n to OpenClaw Control UI by extracting hardcoded English strings into locale files and adding Simplified Chinese (zh-CN) plus a language selector/auto-detect.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: summarizes extracting hardcoded UI strings into locale files, adding Simplified Chinese (zh-CN) and a language selector/auto-detect without any template noise.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): add systemPrompt support for accounts and groups

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp channel's systemPrompt support was only at the root level, so group chats could not receive per-account or per-group systemPrompt instructions to customize reply behavior.
summary.solution_summary: Extend WhatsApp config/schema to support systemPrompt at channels.whatsapp, accounts.<id>, groups.<groupId> (and wildcard '*'); implement resolution hierarchy where account-level overrides root, group-specific or '*' wildcard is chosen within the active groups map, account 'groups' fully replace root 'groups', and the final injected prompt is account prompt + resolved group prompt separated by a blank line; add tests and docs.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Code and config schema were changed for the WhatsApp channel to add account and group-level systemPrompt resolution, with account merging and group prompt composition implemented; account groups replace root groups; no changes to other channels, hot-reload behavior, or group allowlist/mention gating; tests and documentation included.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add hierarchical WhatsApp systemPrompt resolution: support systemPrompt at root, account, and group (including '*' wildcard) levels; account-level overrides root and account 'groups' replace root 'groups'; resolved prompt is account prompt plus group prompt.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the new hierarchical WhatsApp systemPrompt resolution (root/account/group with wildcard and override/merge behavior) without template noise, so it will cluster correctly with similar changes.

results.title: Fix WhatsApp group append processing and account-scoped gatin

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp group messages delivered via messages.upsert(type="append") were being dropped before entering the inbound/agent processing path, and WhatsApp group policy resolution ignored account-scoped config, causing account-level group allowlist/fallbacks to be missed and valid group messages to be rejected.
summary.solution_summary: Enable append processing only for WhatsApp group messages (keep DM append skipped) and propagate accountId/account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom into WhatsApp group policy resolution so account-scoped settings are applied correctly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Scoped bugfix touching WhatsApp inbound and group-activation code (no config/schema changes, DM append still skipped, no dist/debug noise); targeted tests and traces validate the fix; risk noted that enabling group append could replay backlog messages but mitigated by scope and dedupe.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp group messages sent as messages.upsert(type="append") were skipped before agent processing, and group policy resolution omitted accountId so account-scoped groupAllowFrom/allowFrom were ignored resulting in valid group messages being rejected.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: identifies both the messages.upsert(type="append") handling bug and the missing accountId in group policy resolution that caused account-scoped allowFrom settings to be ignored, which accurately captures the core issue and impact.

results.title: fix(gateway): resolve Canvas 401 behind Tailscale Serve

summary.problem_summary: Canvas/A2UI returned 401 when accessed via Tailscale Serve because client IP resolution failed: resolveClientIp returned undefined for requests from a trusted proxy that omitted forwarding headers, and isLocalDirectRequest did not fall back to the raw socket address when allowRealIpFallback was enabled. This prevented local/direct auth detection and blocked Canvas for Serve users.
summary.solution_summary: Make resolveClientIp return the socket address as a fallback when no forwarding headers are present; update isLocalDirectRequest to check req.socket.remoteAddress when allowRealIpFallback is enabled; gate .ts.net host acceptance behind an existing allowTailscale flag and thread auth.allowTailscale through callers; add tests and docs describing required trustedProxies and allowRealIpFallback settings.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Includes unit tests and log traces showing the regression and fix, docs updated with required config, no new config keys introduced (allowTailscale already exists and defaults to true for tailscale.mode = "serve"), and notes about risks and mitigations; depends on an overlapping PR for allowTailscale gating.
summary.dedupe_summary: Canvas/A2UI 401 when behind Tailscale Serve due to client-IP resolution bug: resolveClientIp returns undefined for direct trusted-proxy requests lacking XFF/X-Real-IP and isLocalDirectRequest fails to fallback to req.socket.remoteAddress with allowRealIpFallback enabled; fixed by using socket-address fallback and gating .ts.net host acceptance behind allowTailscale.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the root cause (client IP resolution and fallback failures) and the fix (socket-address fallback and .ts.net gating) that would reliably cluster with similar Tailscale/Trusted-proxy IP issues.

results.title: fix(security): block non-self messages in WhatsApp self-chat mode

summary.problem_summary: checkInboundAccessControl() computed an isSelfChat heuristic but did not enforce it, allowing non-self WhatsApp messages (group messages, DMs from strangers, broadcasts) to pass through because group policy resolution defaulted to "open". This violates the intended self-chat isolation for accounts configured with explicit selfChatMode: true and lets unwanted messages reach downstream processing.
summary.solution_summary: Add an early-return guard in checkInboundAccessControl that rejects all non-self messages when account.selfChatMode === true, placed before any group/DM policy evaluation; the guard checks the explicit config flag (not the heuristic) and preserves self-messages via isSamePhone. No other channel access control or downstream policy logic was changed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is small and focused to src/web/inbound/access-control.ts, tests pass (existing access-control tests), and the guard can be reverted quickly if needed. The fix intentionally does not apply to auto-detected self-chat heuristic to avoid blocking teammates; fail-closed behavior occurs if selfE164 is null.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp self-chat mode (account.selfChatMode === true) did not block non-self messages because isSelfChat was computed but unused and groupPolicy defaulted to "open"; fix adds an early-return in WhatsApp inbound access control to reject all non-self messages before policy evaluation.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states the bug (self-chat mode failed to block non-self messages due to an unused isSelfChat and default groupPolicy) and the fix (early-return guard in inbound access control), making it ideal for deduplication.

results.title: fix: store group messages from non-allowlisted senders as pending context

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy set to "allowlist", group messages from senders not in groupAllowFrom are dropped at the access-control stage and never reach the mention-gating/context-storage logic, so the agent cannot see those messages as recent context.
summary.solution_summary: Add an opt-in config flag groupContextFromAll (top-level and per-account, default false). When enabled, access-control will mark non-allowlisted group messages as storeForContext, the inbound monitor will mark them contextOnly and skip read receipts, and they will be recorded as pending group context without triggering replies.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Opt-in and backward-compatible (default false); changes localized to WhatsApp inbound pipeline, types, config schema, and auto-reply monitor; tested locally. Potential edge cases around allowlist/phone-number normalization and bypassing existing group gating/state updates that may cause subtle behavior differences.
summary.dedupe_summary: Introduce groupContextFromAll to preserve group messages from non-allowlisted senders as contextOnly/pending context (visible in recent chat context) instead of dropping them when groupPolicy is "allowlist"; context-only messages cannot trigger replies and skip read receipts.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the new feature (groupContextFromAll) and its behavior (preserve non-allowlisted group messages as context-only that can't trigger replies and skip read receipts), suitable for deduplication.

results.title: feat(sessions_spawn): add sessionKey param to reuse sub-agent sessions

summary.problem_summary: sessions_spawn always created a new random-UUID sub-agent session, preventing deterministic reuse of a sub-agent's conversation history and risking cross-agent session injection if an external session identifier were allowed unchecked.
summary.solution_summary: Add an optional sessionKey parameter to sessions_spawn that, when provided, maps short keys to a namespaced session id (agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}), accepts fully-qualified session ids only if their embedded agentId matches the target agent, and falls back to crypto.randomUUID() when omitted; includes tests for deterministic keys, UUID fallback, fully-qualified passthrough, and mismatch rejection.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Tests currently contain a broken import (openclaw-tools.js vs clawdbot-tools.js) that prevents compilation; cross-agent validation has a case-sensitivity bug where embedded agentId is not normalized causing false mismatches; error handling is inconsistent (throws vs returning a forbidden jsonResult), which may alter caller behavior.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add optional sessionKey to sessions_spawn to enable deterministic sub-agent sessions (short keys namespaced to agent:{agentId}:subagent:{key}, fully-qualified keys validated to match target agentId), with fallback to random UUID.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical summary that captures the core change (optional sessionKey, namespacing rules, validation, and UUID fallback) without boilerplate, so it will match similar PRs/issues.

results.title: fix(telegram): auto-detect captionable messages for editMessageCaption

summary.problem_summary: Editing Telegram messages that contain media (photo, video, audio, document, animation) fails because the Telegram adapter calls editMessageText, causing a 400 error 'there is no text in the message to edit' and leaving message(action=edit) requests failing for captionable messages. Affected component: src/telegram/send.ts (editMessageTelegram).
summary.solution_summary: editMessageTelegram now catches the specific 400 error (NO_TEXT_IN_MESSAGE_RE) and retries using editMessageCaption for media messages; buttons-only updates use editMessageReplyMarkup directly; MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED is treated as success and HTML parse fallback preserved. Six unit tests were added to cover retry, buttons-only, error propagation, and MESSAGE_NOT_MODIFIED handling.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is backward-compatible with no interface or config changes and only affects the Telegram channel adapter; six new unit tests added; risk that the regex may not match if Telegram changes the error wording (unmatched errors will continue to propagate as before). Revert by restoring src/telegram/send.ts.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram edit fails for media messages because adapter calls editMessageText (400: 'there is no text in the message to edit'); fix by detecting that error and retrying with editMessageCaption (use editMessageReplyMarkup for buttons-only).

judge.reasoning: Concise summary that clearly states the Telegram API error when editing media captions and the adapter change to detect the 400 and retry with editMessageCaption (and use editMessageReplyMarkup for buttons-only), so it will cluster with similar issues.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): restore config-driven block streaming for WhatsApp delivery

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp integration was hardcoded to disable block streaming (disableBlockStreaming: true), causing all messages to be batched and sent only after the agent finished even when per-account or global block streaming settings requested progressive delivery. This broke expected streaming behavior for users who enabled block streaming.
summary.solution_summary: Restore config-driven block streaming by reading per-account channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming and falling back to agents.defaults.blockStreamingDefault; adjust the deliver callback to only suppress tool payloads (reasoning payloads remain filtered upstream by shouldSuppressReasoningPayload).
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change touches extensions/whatsapp message delivery logic to respect per-account/global blockStreaming settings and limits suppression in the deliver callback to tool payloads; reasoning suppression is unchanged and upstream filtering still prevents leaking thinking content. Tests and live verification show progressive delivery restored and no reasoning/tool leaks; default behavior remains off unless enabled.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp integration ignores blockStreaming config and batches messages because disableBlockStreaming was hardcoded; needs to be made config-driven so per-account/global blockStreaming settings enable progressive block streaming.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: it states that WhatsApp ignored blockStreaming due to a hardcoded disableBlockStreaming and must be made config-driven to respect per-account/global settings and enable progressive streaming.

results.title: fix(telegram): fix gateway hang on video messages and increase media download limit

summary.problem_summary: Sending video/media via the Telegram channel could cause the gateway to hang and become completely unresponsive. Root cause: content-length checks triggered before consuming the HTTP response body, leaving an unconsumed body that causes undici Agent.close()/closeDispatcher to deadlock.
summary.solution_summary: Increase default channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb from 5 to 20; fix closeDispatcher to impose a timeout and fallback to destroy the agent to avoid deadlock; make media download failures non-fatal (still forward text/captions); add timeouts and retry logic to getFile and download operations; strip inbound media path metadata from UI.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Changes affect gateway/orchestration and Telegram integration (not SSRF policy): key files src/infra/net/ssrf.ts and src/telegram/bot.ts; backward compatible and configurable via channels.telegram.mediaMaxMb; tests added and verified on live bot; risk: 3s closeDispatcher timeout may prematurely destroy connections but is reversible and default limit can be lowered to 5 for rollback.
summary.dedupe_summary: Gateway hangs when receiving large Telegram media because content-length rejection leaves the response body unconsumed, causing undici Agent.close()/closeDispatcher to block; fix implemented by raising mediaMaxMb, adding timeout+destroy fallback for dispatcher close, and making media download errors non-fatal with retries.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the root cause (unconsumed response body causing undici Agent/dispatcher deadlock) and the implemented fixes (increase media limit, timeout/destroy fallback, non-fatal downloads with retries), so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: fix(telegram): use type-aware media placeholder for document attachments (#7116)

summary.problem_summary: Telegram integration fallback body text used a hardcoded '<media:image>' placeholder for non-audio media when no caption/text was present, causing document (PDF/DOCX) and other non-photo attachments to be misidentified to agents.
summary.solution_summary: Change resolveTelegramInboundBody to use the already-computed, type-aware placeholder variable so the fallback emits '<media:document>', '<media:video>', etc., and add unit tests verifying placeholders and media handling for document messages.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, scoped bugfix in extensions/telegram: only the agent-facing placeholder text was changed; media download and extraction logic unchanged. New unit tests were added; watch for downstream code that pattern-matches on '<media:image>' in this narrow fallback case.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram inbound body fallback incorrectly emitted '<media:image>' for document and other non-photo media when no caption/text was present; needs to use the type-aware '<media:document>'/'<media:video>' placeholder instead.

judge.reasoning: Concise, technical, and focused on the core bug and intended fix (use type-aware media placeholder instead of hardcoded <media:image>), with no boilerplate or extraneous details.

results.title: media: make MEDIA_MAX_BYTES configurable via media.maxBytes

summary.problem_summary: MEDIA_MAX_BYTES was hardcoded (5 MB) in src/media/store.ts so users cannot change the inbound media staging limit; files larger than 5 MB silently fail to stage into sandboxed agents, preventing processing of common large files like PDFs and high-res images.
summary.solution_summary: Introduce a new optional config field media.maxBytes (integer, default 5 MB, max 500 MB) and replace the hardcoded constant with getMediaMaxBytes()/setMediaMaxBytes() usage; the gateway reads media.maxBytes at boot, error messages now report the configured limit, and default behavior remains 5 MB when unset.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change preserves backward compatibility (default 5 MB and MEDIA_MAX_BYTES exported as deprecated), schema enforces a 500 MB cap, tests pass (56 unit tests), bot review threads were addressed, and the change can be reverted by removing media.maxBytes from config.
summary.dedupe_summary: Hardcoded 5MB media staging limit prevents staging files >5MB into sandboxed agents; add a configurable media.maxBytes (default 5MB, cap 500MB) and replace the constant with runtime-configurable getters/setters.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary that captures the core problem (hardcoded 5MB staging limit) and the precise change (add media.maxBytes config and replace constant with runtime getters/setters), so it will cluster correctly with similar issues.

results.title: fix: pass file metadata to agent when Telegram media download fails

summary.problem_summary: Telegram integration was silently dropping messages when media download failed (due to exceeding mediaMaxMb or Telegram Bot API limits) because the handler returned early; as a result the agent never received the message or ack and users got no indication their file/caption was lost. The affected component is the Telegram bot message handling (bot-handlers.ts), causing complete message loss including captions and text when downloads failed.
summary.solution_summary: On download failure, extract file metadata (name, size, type) from the Telegram message object, build a metadata stub and inject it into msg.text, and allow the message to continue through the normal pipeline instead of returning early; this lets the agent see warnings like "⚠️ [File: ...] (exceeds 5MB download limit)" and respond accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Bugfix in src/telegram/bot-handlers.ts for the Telegram integration; touches error paths for media downloads only (no change to normal <mediaMaxMb path, media group, stickers, or outbound sending). Removes prior immediate reply messages and mutates msg.text only in the error path so downstream consumers receive metadata instead of silent drops; related to issues #24854 and #23452.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram bot silently dropped messages when media downloads failed or exceeded size limits; now a file-metadata stub is injected into msg.text and the message is forwarded so the agent sees the file and reason for failure.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific description of the bug (Telegram media downloads causing silent drops) and the fix (injecting file-metadata into msg.text and forwarding the message) that will match similar issues.

results.title: feat(cli): add --session-key targeting for openclaw agent

summary.problem_summary: The openclaw agent CLI cannot target stable session keys because it only supports --session-id, --to, and --agent; this causes CLI-triggered deliveries to resolve to the agent main session instead of a specific channel session when a stable session key is known externally.
summary.solution_summary: Adds a new --session-key CLI flag in register.agent.ts, extends AgentCliOpts with sessionKey, forwards it into resolveSessionKeyForRequest and the gateway call path (agent-via-gateway), and updates help/validation and tests to ensure sessionKey takes top precedence over session-id, --to, and --agent.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, backward-compatible change (label size: S) with targeted edits to CLI parsing and gateway forwarding, includes unit tests for parsing and forwarding, updated help text and validation, and is safe to merge per author’s test run.
summary.dedupe_summary: Add --session-key option to openclaw agent CLI so callers can target a stable session key (takes precedence over session-id/--to/--agent) and have that key forwarded to session resolution/gateway.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary focusing on the new --session-key CLI flag for openclaw agent, its precedence over other flags, and its effect on session resolution/gateway, with no boilerplate or noise.

results.title: feat: separate group-level allowlist from sender-level command authorization

summary.problem_summary: With groupPolicy: "allowlist" the code currently gates all group interactions by sender phone number (groupAllowFrom), preventing: allowlisting groups by JID while allowing any participant to chat, restricting slash commands to owners, and blocking spam groups without locking approved groups.
summary.solution_summary: When groupPolicy is "allowlist" and a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping exists, inbound access is gated by the group JID list (groups config) while groupAllowFrom continues to control slash command authorization; if no groups mapping exists, behavior falls back to the legacy sender-based filtering.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is localized to src/web/inbound/access-control.ts (uses resolveChannelGroupPolicy), updates src/config/types.whatsapp.ts docs, and adds a new Vitest suite for group allowlist behavior; preserves backward compatibility, depends on PR #3326 to avoid implicit allowlist, and has one minor doc/comment inconsistency noted.
summary.dedupe_summary: Change WhatsApp groupPolicy: "allowlist" semantics so that when a channels.whatsapp.groups mapping is provided, access is decided by allowed group JIDs (allow chat from any participant in approved groups) while groupAllowFrom remains the sender-level gate for slash commands; otherwise fall back to sender-based allowlist.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the behavioral change to WhatsApp group allowlist semantics: use groups JIDs for group-level access, allow any participant to chat in approved groups, keep groupAllowFrom for slash commands, with fallback to sender-based allowlist.

results.title: fix(telegram): propagate mediaMaxMb limit to outbound media reply delivery

summary.problem_summary: Telegram integration ignores the configured mediaMaxMb when sending outbound reply messages: loadWebMedia in the reply delivery path is called without maxBytes and falls back to the 100 MB default, so configured lower limits aren’t enforced for replies.
summary.solution_summary: Thread mediaMaxBytes/mediaMaxMb through the Telegram outbound reply delivery chain (bot.ts → bot-message.ts → bot-message-dispatch.ts → delivery.replies.ts and bot-native-commands.ts) so loadWebMedia receives maxBytes; added unit tests and preserved default behavior when unset.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small, low-risk bugfix confined to Telegram integration files that threads an existing parameter; unit tests added, backward-compatible, easily reversible (single commit), scope labeled small.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram outbound reply delivery does not enforce configured mediaMaxMb because loadWebMedia is called without maxBytes; fix by passing mediaMaxBytes through the bot → bot-message → dispatch → delivery.replies (and native-commands) path so replies honor the configured limit.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug (outbound replies ignore mediaMaxMb) and the targeted fix (thread mediaMaxBytes through the delivery path), with no template noise, so it will cluster correctly.

results.title: fix(telegram): add HTML parse fallback for media captions

summary.problem_summary: Telegram media send endpoints (sendPhoto, sendDocument, sendVideo, sendAnimation, sendAudio, sendVoice, video note) would fail with a 400 "can't parse entities" when captions contained invalid/mismatched HTML entities because they lacked the HTML-parse fallback used by text messages.
summary.solution_summary: Wrap the media send callback in withTelegramHtmlParseFallback so that on an HTML parse error the send is retried without parse_mode and with the raw plain-text caption; applies to all media types listed.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Bug fix in src/telegram/send.ts, adds one unit test verifying retry behavior; change is backward-compatible, limited to media send inner callback (text/edit/thread/caption-splitting unchanged), and can be reverted by restoring send.ts; tests pass.
summary.dedupe_summary: Telegram media sends fail with 400 "can't parse entities" for captions containing invalid HTML because media send path lacks the HTML parse fallback; fix retries the media send without parse_mode using the raw caption.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug (Telegram media captions failing with HTML parse errors) and the precise fix (retry without parse_mode using raw caption), with no boilerplate or irrelevant details.

results.title: fix(whatsapp): make block streaming configurable and fix delivery race in block-reply pipeline

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp channel had block streaming effectively disabled and a delivery race: intermediate block replies were enqueued optimistically and the pipeline marked them as sent before actual delivery, which could suppress final replies and cause silent turns.
summary.solution_summary: Make block streaming configurable via channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming, fix the boolean activation bug, add sensible coalescing defaults, implement dispatcher.sendBlockReplyAsync that awaits confirmed delivery (rejecting on failure), and update onBlockReply to await it so sentContentKeys are only set after successful delivery; tests updated accordingly.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: This changes WhatsApp message processing and the reply dispatcher API (adds sendBlockReplyAsync); maintainers must update mocks/dispatchers and can configure blockStreaming per-account or globally. Tests were added/updated to cover streaming on/off paths and delivery failure handling.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp block-reply streaming was disabled and suffered a race where block replies were optimistically enqueued and marked sent before actual delivery, suppressing final replies; fix by making blockStreaming configurable and awaiting confirmed block delivery (sendBlockReplyAsync) before marking content as sent.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the core bug (optimistic enqueue/race suppressing final replies) and the fix (make blockStreaming configurable and await confirmed block delivery via sendBlockReplyAsync), with no extraneous boilerplate.

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Introduce group-level allowlist support for the Signal channel so specific Signal groups (by ID) can be allowed regardless of sender-level groupAllowFrom checks; this affects Signal config, schema, event handling, and command authorization, enabling whole groups to receive messages and run control commands.
summary.solution_summary: Add a SignalGroupConfig type and a channels.signal.groups.<groupId> map to SignalAccountConfig and Zod schema; wire resolveChannelGroupPolicy into the Signal event handler and commandGate authorizers so listed groups bypass sender-level checks and are authorized for control commands; update docs.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Implementation follows existing patterns from other channels, addresses Greptile review feedback (ensuring allowed groups can run commands), passes TypeScript checks, supersedes PR #4337, and fixes issue #25540; safe to merge with low risk.
summary.dedupe_summary: Signal channel: add channels.signal.groups.<groupId> allowlist so listed groups bypass sender-level groupAllowFrom and are authorized for messages and control commands.

judge.reasoning: Concise, focused summary that names the specific config key and clearly states the functional change (group-level allowlist bypassing sender-level checks for messages and commands).

results.title: fix(signal): add group-level allowlist support via groups config

summary.problem_summary: Signal channel lacked group-level allowlist support: when groupPolicy was set to "allowlist" and groupAllowFrom contained group IDs, group messages were silently dropped because groupAllowFrom is a sender-level filter and never matches a group ID.
summary.solution_summary: Added a groups config to SignalAccountSchemaBase and invoked resolveChannelGroupPolicy in Signal's group access check before falling back to sender-level groupAllowFrom; added four tests to cover group-level policy resolution.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Small Signal-specific fix (schema + event-handler change) with 4 new tests; all config tests pass and it resolves silent-dropping of group messages under groupPolicy="allowlist" (fixes the reported issue).
summary.dedupe_summary: Signal ignores group-level allowlists: group IDs placed in groupAllowFrom are treated as sender identifiers so group messages are dropped; missing channels.signal.groups config and resolveChannelGroupPolicy call caused the bug.

judge.reasoning: Concise and specific: explains that Signal treated group IDs as sender IDs causing dropped group messages and notes the missing channels.signal.groups config/resolveChannelGroupPolicy, which is exactly the core bug for deduplication.

results.title: feat(whatsapp): support config-driven block streaming

summary.problem_summary: WhatsApp channel always coalesces agent output into a single final message because process-message.ts hardcodes disableBlockStreaming: true, so intermediate blocks (tool outputs/sleeps) are not delivered as separate messages.
summary.solution_summary: Make WhatsApp respect the channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming config by adding resolveWhatsAppBlockStreamingEnabled(), replacing the hardcoded flag with the config-driven value, treating intermediate blocks as valid replies when blockStreaming is enabled, registering the capability and default in the channel dock, and updating tests.
summary.maintainer_signal_summary: Change is small and backwards-compatible (default behavior remains coalescing when blockStreaming is false/omitted), tests updated to cover enabled/disabled behavior, registers new capability and default constant in dock, and fixes queuedFinal logic to match Telegram/Line behavior; labeled channel: whatsapp-web, app: web-ui, agents, size: S.
summary.dedupe_summary: WhatsApp channel hardcodes disableBlockStreaming:true causing all message blocks to be batched; should instead respect channels.whatsapp.blockStreaming so intermediate blocks can be delivered as separate WhatsApp messages when enabled.

judge.reasoning: Concise, specific summary of the bug and intended config-driven change without boilerplate or extraneous details, so it will cluster with similar issues.
